questions.text: I find that I automatically catalog the relationships between people when I enter a new social group.
I develop skills when I actually need them rather than anticipating future requirements.
I am the kind of person others describe as unflappable.
I sense when someone gives a longer answer than the question warranted because they are nervous.
I prefer to offer suggestions rather than give directives.
I find that the moral demand of suffering is the experience that most consistently drives me to reconsider whether I am doing enough.
I can distinguish between understanding that someone is in pain and actually feeling that pain myself — and I tend toward the former.
I find that the thought of others benefiting from my effort is the strongest motivation I know.
I feel that my exercise time, my meal preparation, and my rest are boundaries I protect fiercely.
I feel most at ease when I am the one setting expectations.
I notice that my heart rate stays fairly constant even during heated discussions.
I am at my best when I am in the driver's seat.
I find that I can move through emotionally difficult conversations without carrying the weight of them afterward.
I believe that looking after my own needs is the foundation of a sustainable life.
I prefer environments where norms and rules are stated outright.
I often sense what someone really means even when their words say something different.
I set reminders and alarms to keep myself on track throughout the day.
I do not naturally detect when someone's storytelling style changes depending on the status of their audience.
I believe that the moral priority of suffering is the principle that should guide not only individual conduct but also the design of social systems.
I focus all my energy on the primary plan rather than diverting effort to contingencies.
I feel a surge of energy when a team collectively discovers its new direction.
I find that emotional recovery is a slow process for me compared to others.
I feel empowered when the group trusts me enough to share power.
I often feel like I'm vibrating at a higher frequency than the situation warrants.
I rarely register the social significance of response time in digital conversations.
I believe that the professionals and leaders who make the greatest positive impact are those who can process suffering without emotional disruption.
I tend to put other people's needs ahead of my own, even when it costs me.
I feel that waiting for full consensus wastes valuable momentum.
I hold the view that the moral significance of suffering should be the primary consideration when I am deciding how to act.
My system seems to auto-regulate emotional intensity without requiring conscious effort.
I allocate specific blocks of time for different activities throughout my day.
I hold the view that my own legacy—what I build and leave behind—is a primary concern.
I hold the view that the proper response to receiving help is to immediately look for someone else to help.
I find that one strong emotion can completely hijack my attention.
I lay out my clothes, pack my bag, or prepare materials the night before.
I read whatever catches my interest without following a list.
